subject: Cloud Computing
title: SLAs: The Uptime Promise
description: A micro-lesson that makes SLAs: The Uptime Promise usable.

hook.text: Cloud providers promise 99.99% uptime. That sounds perfect — but it still means 52 minutes of downtime a year.

buildup.text: Service Level Agreements spell out exactly how much uptime the provider guarantees.

discovery.text: If they miss the target, you get credits — not cash. Usually just a discount on next month's bill.

twist.text: SLA credits rarely cover real losses. An hour of downtime can cost a business millions in revenue.

climax.text: Smart teams design for failure, not uptime promises. SLAs are safety nets, not guarantees.

punchline.text: Don't trust the SLA. Design as if the cloud will fail — because it will.

quiz.question: What happens if a cloud provider misses its SLA uptime target?
options: Customers typically receive billing credits, not full compensation
The provider must pay for all business losses caused by downtime
Affected customers automatically receive free service for a year
